1: He made the altar of burnt offering (mizbeach ha-olah) of acacia wood: Mizbeach ha-olah = the altar of the whole burnt offering (olah = that which goes up; the completely-consumed sacrifice). This altar is the first object encountered after passing through the court entrance — before the Holy Place, before the laver, the worshipper passes the altar of sacrifice. Access to the divine presence begins with blood. The altar's position at the entry is the spatial theology of atonement.
2: He made its horns (qarnotav) on its four corners; its horns were of one piece with it (miqqshah), and he overlaid it with bronze: Qernot = horns (the projections at the four top corners of the altar; from qeren = horn/power/ray). The horns are structural and symbolic: blood is applied to the horns in the atonement rituals (Lev 4:7, 8:15); fleeing criminals grasped the altar horns for sanctuary (1 Kgs 1:50-51). Miqqshah again: the horns are not attached to the altar but hammered out of the same metal structure as the altar. Bronze (n'choshet) = the outer court metal; the transition from gold (inner furnishings) to bronze (outer court) is complete.
3: He made all the utensils of the altar, the pots (sirot), the shovels (ya'im), the basins (mizraqot), the forks (mizlagot), and the fire pans (machtot). He made all its utensils of bronze: The complete bronze service kit for the burnt-offering altar: sirot = cooking pots (for boiling portions); ya'im = shovels (for removing ashes); mizraqot = basins for blood (from zaraq = to sprinkle/throw; the same verb as Moses throwing covenant blood on the people in 24:8); mizlagot = flesh-hooks/forks (for handling the sacrificial portions); machtot = fire-pans (for carrying coals). Every piece of hardware is bronze — the altar's service set matches its material.
4: He made for the altar a grating (mikbar r'shet n'choshet), a network of bronze, under its ledge (karkav), extending halfway down: Mikbar = a grating/lattice (from a root meaning to make holes); r'shet = network/mesh; the bronze grating sits halfway down the altar, providing a shelf for the sacrifice and allowing air flow for combustion. Karkav = ledge/projection (a technical architectural term for a horizontal projection in a wall or structure). The grating's position halfway down the hollow altar creates a functional firebox — the bronze mesh holds the sacrifice while flames burn below.
5: He cast four rings on the four corners of the bronze grating as holders for the poles: The grating itself has four rings for poles — an inner carrying system for the grating inside the altar. Or the four rings are at the altar's corners at the grating level, serving as the carrying rings for the entire altar via poles inserted through the grating. The precise mechanics of assembly are described so that any future Levitical generation can reconstruct the altar correctly.
6: He made the poles of acacia wood and overlaid them with bronze: The altar poles follow the standard protocol: acacia wood + metal overlay. But the overlay here is bronze (not gold or silver) — the altar's poles match the altar's metal. The gradation of materials is maintained even for transport accessories.
7: And he put the poles through the rings on the sides of the altar to carry it with them. He made it hollow, with boards (n'vuv luchot asah oto): N'vuv = hollow (from navav = to hollow out/bore); luchot = boards/planks; the altar is hollow — not a solid block of acacia but a box made of boards, overlaid with bronze. The hollow construction serves two purposes: portability (solid acacia + bronze would be immovably heavy) and function (the hollow interior is filled with earth or stones at each campsite per 20:24-25, creating the sacrificial fire base on fresh ground).
8: He made the basin (kiyyor) of bronze and its stand (kano) of bronze, from the mirrors (mar'ot) of the ministering women who ministered (tzov'ot asher tzav'u) at the entrance of the tent of meeting: Kiyyor = basin/laver (used for washing); kano = its stand/pedestal. Mar'ot = mirrors (polished bronze mirrors — ancient mirrors were not glass but highly polished bronze or copper, which the women gave up for the laver); tzov'ot asher tzav'u = the ministering women who served (the same root tzava twice — they are army women, those who served in organized corps). These women appear nowhere else in the Pentateuch; they give up their personal mirrors — instruments of vanity and beauty — to make the vessel of priestly purification. The material transformation (beauty instrument → cleansing vessel) is itself a theological symbol: what was used for self-presentation becomes the means of purification for approach to God.
9: He made the court (chatzer). For the south side the hangings of the court were of fine twined linen (shesh moshzar), a hundred cubits: Chatzer = court/enclosure (also the word for a village or unwalled settlement — a defined but open space). The court is the outermost sacred space, accessible to lay Israelites who are not priests. The court hangings are fine linen but not the colored, embroidered linen of the interior — the court maintains holiness but not the same degree as the tabernacle itself. A hundred cubits = approximately 150 feet — the full length of the southern boundary.
10: Their twenty pillars and twenty bases were of bronze, but the hooks of the pillars and their fillets were of silver: The court pillars rest on bronze bases (the outer material) but their connecting hardware (hooks and fillets) is silver — the court is a transition zone between the fully bronze outer world and the silver-based inner structure. The material gradient: outside world (no metal) → court (bronze bases, silver hardware) → tabernacle walls (silver bases) → inner furnishings (gold).
11: For the north side there were hangings of a hundred cubits, their twenty pillars, twenty bases of bronze, with hooks and fillets of silver: The north wall mirrors the south wall exactly: same hanging length, same pillar count, same base material, same hardware material. The court's north and south walls are symmetrical — bilateral order in the sacred space.
12: For the west side there were hangings of fifty cubits, their ten pillars, and ten bases, with hooks and fillets of silver: The west end of the court: fifty cubits wide (the court is half as wide as it is long — 100 cubits by 50 cubits in total). Ten pillars at fifty cubits = five cubits per pillar spacing, consistent with the east and north/south spacing.
13: For the front to the east, fifty cubits. The court entrance faces east — the direction of sunrise, the direction from which the camp approaches. The east end is fifty cubits wide (the same as the west), divided into two screen panels flanking the entrance gate.
14: The hangings for one side of the gate were fifteen cubits, with their three pillars and three bases: The gate section: the overall fifty-cubit east end is divided as follows: 15 cubits of screen (south of the gate) + 20 cubits of gate opening + 15 cubits of screen (north of the gate) = 50 cubits. The gate opening (20 cubits wide) is the widest single opening in the tabernacle complex — welcoming, accessible, the point of entry for the whole congregation.
15: And so for the other side. On both sides of the gate of the court were hangings of fifteen cubits, with their three pillars and three bases: The symmetry of the gate flanks: 15 cubits + 3 pillars on each side. The gate is framed, not fenced off — its design creates a portal of invitation, not a barrier. The screen of the gate (masach sha'ar hechatzer) is the first of three thresholds: court gate → tabernacle entrance → inner veil.
16: All the hangings around the court were of fine twined linen: Despite using only one color and lacking the cherubim motif of the inner curtains, the court hangings are fine linen throughout — the entire court space is marked as sacred. Even the most accessible zone of the tabernacle complex uses premium material.
17: The bases for the pillars were of bronze, the hooks of the pillars and their fillets were of silver, and the overlaying of their capitals was of silver, and all the pillars of the court were filleted with silver: The systematic description of the court pillar hardware: bronze bases, silver hooks and bands, silver-overlaid capitals. The silver binding of all court pillars creates a visual cohesion — all the court pillars gleam with silver bands, visually unifying the sacred space.
18: The screen for the gate of the court was embroidered with needlework in blue and purple and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen (roqem): The entrance gate screen is the most elaborate element of the court — embroidered with all four colors (blue, purple, scarlet, linen), using the roqem technique. The gate is the most decorated element of the court, befitting its role as the threshold of transition from the profane world to the sacred space.
19: It was twenty cubits long and five cubits high in its breadth, corresponding to the hangings of the court: The gate screen is twenty cubits wide and five cubits high — the full width of the entrance opening and the height of the court walls. The gate screen fills the entire entrance portal.
20: And their four pillars and their four bases were of bronze; their hooks were of silver, and the overlaying of their capitals and their fillets were of silver: The gate screen's four pillars follow the court pattern: bronze bases, silver hardware. The gate's four pillars (vs. three for each flanking panel) mark the entrance as architecturally distinct — four is the number of completeness at the corners of a portal.
21: All the pegs (yetedot) for the tabernacle and for the court all around were of bronze: Yetedot = pegs/stakes (tent pegs that anchor the guy-ropes of the tabernacle and court hangings); the pegs are all bronze — the outermost attachment point of the structure uses the court material. The hierarchy bottoms out at bronze for the ground-level anchors.
22: These are the recorded accounts (p'quddei) of the tabernacle, the tabernacle of the testimony (mishkan ha'edut): Paquddei = the records/accounts/accounts (from paqad = to number/register/visit/oversee); the same root as the census (p'qudim = the numbered). Mishkan ha'edut = tabernacle of the testimony/covenant (edut = testimony, referring to the tablets of the testimony in the ark). The tabernacle is defined by what it houses: the testimony, the covenant record.
23: As Moses commanded, according to the service of the Levites, under the direction of Ithamar the son of Aaron the priest: Ithamar (the fourth son of Aaron) has supervisory responsibility for the tabernacle accounts — an early indication of his administrative role. The Levitical service (avodah) framework for tabernacle maintenance is established here; Numbers 4 will specify each Levitical clan's transport duties in detail.
24: Bezalel the son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, made all that YHWH commanded Moses: The formal credit line: Bezalel is identified by father (Uri), grandfather (Hur), and tribe (Judah). Hur is the companion of Aaron at Rephidim who held Moses's arms during the battle (17:12); he may be Miriam's son. The Judah-tribe identification matters for later tradition: the artisan who built the divine dwelling is from the royal tribe.
25: And with him was Oholiab the son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan, a craftsman and designer and embroiderer in blue and purple and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen: Oholiab (Oholi-av = 'my father is my tent' or 'tent of the father') is from Dan — the northernmost tribe. The pairing of Bezalel (Judah, south) and Oholiab (Dan, north) represents the full geographic and tribal breadth of Israel contributing to the tabernacle construction. Together they represent the whole people.
26: All the gold that was used for the work, in all the construction of the sanctuary, the gold from the offering, was twenty-nine talents and seven hundred and thirty shekels, by the shekel of the sanctuary: The gold inventory: 29 talents + 730 shekels. A talent = approximately 75 pounds = approximately 34 kg; so approximately 2,175 pounds = nearly a ton of gold in the entire tabernacle. The accounting is exact: the gold total is tallied to the shekel (the shekel of the sanctuary = a standard weight kept at the tabernacle to prevent fraud — the sanctuary maintained the reference standard for commerce).
27: The silver from those of the congregation who were numbered was a hundred talents and 1,775 shekels, by the shekel of the sanctuary: The silver total of 100 talents + 1,775 shekels came entirely from the census half-shekels (v.26 = the ransom of those numbered). One hundred talents = 100 silver bases for the tabernacle frames and inner veil pillars; 1,775 shekels = the hooks, fillets, and overlays for the court pillars. Every ounce of structural silver in the tabernacle represents a numbered Israelite — the tabernacle's foundations literally consist of the registered lives of the people of God.
28: He made the hooks of the pillars and overlaid their capitals and filleted them with it: The 1,775 remaining shekels of silver (after making the 100 bases) went entirely to the court pillar hardware: the hooks, the capital overlays, and the connecting fillets. Nothing was wasted; the census surplus accounts were precisely expended.
29: The bronze of the offering was seventy talents and 2,400 shekels: The bronze total: 70 talents + 2,400 shekels — contributed as a freewill offering (not from the census; the census silver was the ransom payment). Bronze was contributed voluntarily; silver was a required payment. The material hierarchy corresponds to the spiritual hierarchy: gold (most valuable, inner furnishings), silver (census-ransom, structural foundations), bronze (freewill, outer court and practical implements).
30: With it he made the bases for the entrance of the tent of meeting, the bronze altar and the bronze grating for it, and all the utensils of the altar: The complete inventory of what bronze was used for: the entrance bases (5 for the entrance screen pillars), the altar and its grating, and all the altar's service utensils. The bronze goes first and primarily to the sacrificial apparatus — the altar, its tools, its support structure.
31: The bases of the court all around, and the bases of the gate of the court, and all the pegs of the tabernacle, and all the pegs of the court all around: The final bronze allocation: all the court bases (56 bases for the 56 court pillars), the gate screen bases (4), and every tent peg throughout the entire complex. The pegs (yetedot) that literally hold the tabernacle to the ground are bronze — the material that connects the holy structure to the earth is the most utilitarian metal, the one farthest from gold.
